headline: Week 2 of Operation Epic Fury: No Ceasefire, No Endgame, No Off-Ramp
subheadline: With 140 US troops injured, Kurdish forces advancing into Iranian territory, and ground invasion not ruled out, the conflict is escalating beyond any scenario Washington initially planned for

hero_svg_alt: Abstract visualization of escalating conflict: ascending angular red forms representing intensifying military operations, connected by a dashed gold trajectory line showing escalation path

body.content: Ten days into Operation Epic Fury, the US-Israeli war against Iran has entered a phase that military planners in Washington did not publicly anticipate: a grinding, multi-front campaign with no ceasefire in sight, 140 American service members injured, Kurdish proxy forces advancing on the ground, and a White House that refuses to rule out a full-scale ground invasion. The IRGC, meanwhile, has declared that Tehran -- not Washington -- will decide when this war ends.

body.heading: The Escalation Spiral Accelerates
body.content: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's pledge of the 'most intense day yet' of US strikes encapsulates the trajectory of a conflict that has defied early predictions of a swift, limited campaign. The Pentagon confirmed that 140 US troops have been injured in the first ten days of fighting, with eight classified as severe. These figures, while modest by historical standards of major conflicts, represent the highest US military casualty rate since the early months of the Iraq war in 2003. The operational tempo is staggering. US and Israeli aircraft have conducted thousands of sorties targeting IRGC command infrastructure, air defense networks, nuclear facilities, and now increasingly, civilian dual-use infrastructure. Tehran residents described sustained bombardment that left 'hearts shaking' as the capital endured around-the-clock strikes. Iran's internet has been shut down nationwide, severing the population's connection to the outside world and making independent damage assessment virtually impossible. The humanitarian toll is mounting. The World Health Organization has verified 13 attacks on health facilities inside Iran. A US-Israeli missile strike near a girls' school drew international condemnation, with the Trump administration stating it was 'willing to live with' the report -- a response that has fueled diplomatic backlash from European allies and non-aligned nations.

body.heading: The Kurdish Ground Dimension Changes Everything
body.content: The most consequential development of Week 2 is the opening of a ground front. President Trump confirmed that US jets would provide air cover for Kurdish forces advancing into Iranian territory from Iraq's Kurdistan region. This proxy ground offensive fundamentally alters the character of the conflict. Air campaigns, however intense, have natural limitations. They degrade military infrastructure and command networks but cannot hold territory or force regime capitulation. The Kurdish advance introduces territorial ambiguity -- the prospect that parts of western Iran could come under insurgent control, creating a new set of political and military dynamics that extend the conflict's duration well beyond what an air-only campaign would produce. For Iran, the Kurdish front is existential in a way that aerial bombardment is not. Tehran has fought Kurdish separatism for decades, and the specter of US-backed Kurdish territorial gains in western Iran threatens the country's territorial integrity in a manner that could outlast any ceasefire. The IRGC's response has been to redouble its commitment: senior commanders declared that Tehran will determine the war's endpoint, signaling no interest in negotiations under current conditions. The White House's refusal to rule out boots on the ground adds another escalation vector. US ground forces in Iran would transform this from an aerial campaign with proxy elements into a full-scale ground war -- a scenario that would require Congressional authorization, dramatically increase costs, and potentially trigger the kind of regional conflagration that even hawkish planners in Washington have sought to avoid.

body.heading: An Economy Approaching State Failure
body.content: Iran's economic data, already unreliable under years of sanctions, has become nearly meaningless in wartime conditions. The Stratoterra model estimates GDP on a trajectory of -15% to -20% annualized contraction. Consumer price inflation, last officially measured at 32.5%, is now projected to exceed 50% and accelerating toward hyperinflationary thresholds. The rial's parallel market rate has collapsed, making the official exchange rate functionally irrelevant. The country's primary revenue source -- oil exports worth approximately $112 billion annually -- has been physically severed by the mining of the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's current account, previously in modest surplus at 1.8% of GDP, is swinging deeply negative as export capacity drops to near zero while wartime imports continue via overland routes. Government debt, at 35.6% of GDP before the war, is exploding as emergency military spending surges against a collapsing revenue base. Foreign exchange reserves are being consumed at an unsustainable rate. The IRGC Interim Leadership Council faces an impossible fiscal equation: fund the war effort or maintain basic civilian services. Historical precedent suggests they will choose the former.

body.heading: The Investor Calculus: Duration Is the Variable That Matters
body.content: For global markets, Iran itself is irrelevant as an investment destination -- it has been effectively uninvestable for years under the sanctions regime. What matters is the war's duration and its cascading effects on energy prices, shipping routes, and risk sentiment. Every additional week of conflict compounds the damage. Brent crude at $116.50 per barrel is already inflicting pain on energy-importing economies. Japan, with just 10% energy independence and massive Hormuz exposure, faces a GDP growth trajectory that has shifted from near-zero to negative. South Korea, routing 70% of its oil imports through the strait, confronts a similar deterioration. Germany's already-negative growth rate of -0.5% is worsening as energy costs surge. The US itself, despite relative energy self-sufficiency, is not immune. GDP growth of 2.8% faces headwinds from the oil shock feeding through transportation and logistics costs. More critically, the war spending layered on top of the $175 billion SCOTUS tariff refund liability is pushing government debt -- already at 118% of GDP -- on a trajectory that bond markets are beginning to price. The $1 trillion leveraged basis trade in Treasuries has started unwinding, recalling the March 2020 Treasury market seizure. The Dow's 800-point decline reflects not just Iran war jitters but a growing recognition that the US faces a multi-front fiscal crisis with no clear resolution. Defense stocks will continue to outperform, but the broader market faces a prolonged risk-off environment that will not resolve until one of two things happens: a ceasefire, or a market consensus that the conflict's economic damage has been fully priced.

body.heading: What to Watch This Week
body.content: Three developments will determine whether this conflict escalates further or begins to plateau. First, the Kurdish ground offensive's progress: if proxy forces take and hold Iranian territory, the political dynamics shift irreversibly toward a longer war. Second, the Fed's March 19 meeting: how the Federal Reserve responds to the stagflationary impulse from the oil shock will set the tone for global monetary policy. Hold rates and risk inflation embedding; cut rates and risk credibility; raise rates and risk recession. Every option is bad. Third, the IRGC's Hormuz capability: Iran's ability to maintain the mine barrier under sustained US naval pressure will determine whether the energy disruption lasts weeks or months. Satellite imagery of mine-clearing operations in the strait is the single most important data point for energy markets right now. For investors, the uncomfortable reality is that this war has no visible off-ramp. Washington is escalating, Tehran is entrenching, and the proxy ground dimension ensures that even a ceasefire will not produce a clean resolution. Position accordingly.
